nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich sheltered coastal reefs; associated with algae and staghorn/dead coral sections at depths of 1–15 m

diet.notes: Has been documented accepting meaty foods, herbivore flakes, and frozen preparations. Generally adapts well to prepared aquarium foods. Wild diet consists of zooplankton and algae.

summary: The Ternate Damselfish is a small reef fish from sheltered Indo-Pacific coral reefs, generally considered reef-safe and one of the easier damselfish to keep. It tends to become territorial as it matures, particularly toward other damselfish and similarly sized tankmates.
careNotes:
The Ternate Damselfish (Amblyglyphidodon ternatensis) is generally considered one of the more manageable damselfish for beginner marine aquarists, though it has been reported to become increasingly territorial as it matures, particularly toward other damselfish and similarly sized tankmates. Providing plenty of live rock with multiple caves, crevices, and distinct hiding spots is widely advised to reduce aggression by giving each fish its own defined space.

Adults have been documented aggregating with the closely related Amblyglyphidodon curacao in the wild, though caution is still advisable when mixing damselfish in captivity. The Ternate Damselfish is presently uncommon in the aquarium trade and may require sourcing from specialty marine retailers.
breedingNotes: The Ternate Damselfish has been reported to breed in captivity in a manner similar to other damselfish. A bonded pair claims a small territory and deposits eggs on cleared flat surfaces — typically dead coral branches or rubble — after preparing the site. The male then guards and aerates the eggs until they hatch. Breeding pairs tend to become notably more aggressive toward tankmates during the spawning period, so extra hiding spots and careful observation are advisable at that time.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-layer (oviparous). Pairs deposit eggs on dead coral or rubble, and the male guards and aerates the clutch until hatching. Has been reported to breed in suitably sized reef aquariums.
